chapters.title: No woman is left alone during pregnancy
chapters.text:
Pregnancy is a momentous chapter in a woman's life, where every choice feels like a huge decision. It's a time when advice and guidance come from everywhere, often overwhelming new mothers.

For every new life stage, knowledge becomes an essential asset that helps navigate the onset of challenges. And it's precisely this wisdom that Emily Oster has set out to share with us. She challenges the common knowledge about pregnancy and proves it's not a one-size-fits-all experience.

Women must have the right to control their childbearing gift.

Pregnancy advice has long been shrouded in myth and superstition, leaving expectant mothers to wonder what's safe and what's not. Armed with the power of data and evidence, Oster dismantles these myths one by one. She empowers us to make informed decisions while allowing women to regain control over their bodies. Unfortunately, society still takes childbearing and the subsequent physical and mental changes for granted, leaving women to handle them alone.

Pregnancy and childbirth (and child-rearing) are among the most important and meaningful experiences most of us will ever have. ~ Emily Oster PhD

However, bearing a child is a unique experience, full of individual circumstances, desires, and priorities. There is no one right path and no universal set of rules. Oster encourages future mothers to balance risks and benefits and make choices that align with their values. From caffeine consumption to prenatal testing, she provides the clarity and transparency needed to make decisions that resonate individually.

So, let's start this sprint with an open heart and a curious mind. After all, it can't be scarier than giving birth, right? Let’s find out!

chapters.title: Get intimate with your cervix
chapters.text:
A common misconception is that parenthood starts with your baby's first cry. However, this is far from the truth: you become a parent even before discovering you're about to be one, right at the time of conception. No wonder this phase is marked by questions, preparation, and scientific inquiry.

The most urgent query most partners often have is: “What is the best age to try for a baby?” These numbers often play a significant role for those planning to conceive. After all, we've all heard about the dreaded “advanced maternal age” label, typically applied to those over 35. But here's the truth: fertility doesn't adhere to a strict timetable. Every person's fertility experience is as distinct as their fingerprint.

That said, the next question on the list is regarding contraception. For many years, using contraceptives to avoid pregnancy has become a part of many couples’ routines. Shifting from this mindset to actively attempting to conceive is a significant change. When going through this shift, be patient with yourself and your body; pregnancy might not happen instantly.

Before trying for a baby, both partners should assess their medical history and overall health.

Understanding your body is paramount on this path, especially regarding ovulation. Envision your body as a finely tuned instrument and ovulation as the precise note you need to hit. Timing is vital, but it's not the sole factor. Both partners' overall health and well-being also contribute to this intricate process.

Some ways to monitor ovulation include:

• Tracking your body temperature: Ovulation causes a slight temperature increase until your next period. Take notice to calculate the best time for conception.
• Monitoring your cervical mucus: During ovulation, your cervix releases egg-white, sticky mucus to ease the movement of sperm. Use this information for a more accurate prediction.
• Using ovulation sticks: These are relatively new, but you can try them to detect the luteinizing hormone typically released during ovulation.

Along with “fine-tuning” your menstrual cycle comes the “two-week wait,” when you might find yourself holding your breath, hoping for a positive pregnancy test. Many women act like they're already pregnant during this period, steering clear of caffeine, alcohol, and particular foods. But remember, life happens; you may slip up and enjoy that cup of coffee or a glass of wine. Don't be excessively harsh on yourself; stress isn't your ally during this time.

chapters.title: The rules of early pregnancy
chapters.text:
The first trimester can be terrifying — the uncertainty and doubt can stress out even the most level-headed future parents. Let's look at what can make this process easier and safer.

First, eating habits play a crucial role in safe pregnancy. Caffeine, alcohol, and tobacco are infamous vices every pregnant woman must diligently avoid. The good news is that moderate caffeine or light alcohol consumption is generally considered safe. However, heavy drinking is unquestionably off-limits during the rest of the pregnancy.

While your appetite may increase, remember that you're not “eating for two.” Prioritize the quality of your food intake over quantity. Write down the following products to avoid during pregnancy:

• Raw meat
• Unwashed produce
• Unpasteurized milk and plain soft cheese
• Deli meals
• Raw and smoked fish and shellfish (but sushi and eggs are safe)

Speaking of fish, not every kind is safe, no matter how you cook it. The reason is mercury, which can harm the fetus's brain development. However, options like salmon, pollock, or herring are better because they are high in omega-3 fatty acids, healthy for the baby, and low in mercury. On the other hand, avoid canned tuna, king mackerel, and especially orange roughy.

Myths and superstitions can cause more stress and damage than actual pregnancy risks.

Another frequent cause for concern during this phase is morning sickness. Its frequency and intensity can vary from woman to woman, but it’s one of the most common companions of the first trimester. Some choose to endure extreme nausea, while other women turn to medications. Though most are safe, consulting your doctor is always the best precaution.

Yet another common fear in the early stages of pregnancy is miscarriage, even though most result from chromosomal abnormalities, not the mother's actions. If you’re worried about this, consider prenatal screening for chromosomal abnormalities. Some tests involve non-invasive methods like blood tests and ultrasounds. In contrast, others, such as amniocentesis or CVS, are invasive and carry a slight risk. Your doctor will recommend the best course of action for you.

Now let's address the worries many mothers-to-be are embarrassed to ask about or simply unaware of:

• Changing cat litter is usually okay, but always wash your hands thoroughly.
• Gardening can raise the risk of toxoplasmosis, so it is best to avoid it.
• Dying your hair is generally safe but choose products with fewer chemicals.
• Avoid getting too hot during the first trimester, whether due to fever, hot tubs, or intense yoga. It can elevate the risk of neural tube defects.

chapters.title: Keeping up with your body
chapters.text:
Navigating the second trimester of pregnancy is a peculiar experience. You will start to notice changes that weren't there yesterday and learn to adapt to them.

Many pregnant women start to do light physical exercise to stay in shape and relieve future labor. Indeed, maintaining an active lifestyle during pregnancy can be advantageous, but always listen to your body.

• Avoid traditional sit-ups and crunches after 20 weeks due to the risk of decreased blood flow to the fetus and the separation of abdominal muscles.
• Don't attempt activities like skiing, climbing, or football due to the risk of falling and placenta detachment.

On the other hand, not exercising is an option, too, but wait until we introduce you to some godsend activities for pregnant women:

• Kegels, or pelvic floor exercises, strengthen the birthing muscles and, thus, ease pain during labor. They also help avoid urinary problems after delivery.
• Prenatal yoga is reportedly effective for relieving first-stage labor pain. Moreover, some women even noted shorter labor duration.

Pregnancy is always an adjustment, so pay attention to your body’s subtle signs.

Illnesses don't disappear during pregnancy; aside from physical activity, you should be mindful of what you put in your body. Medication choices require careful consideration as most drugs can pass through the placenta, potentially affecting the fetus.

The FDA categorizes drugs into safety classes (A, B, C, D, X) during pregnancy, with categories A and B generally considered safe, while D and X may potentially harm babies. As for category C, additional consultation may be a good idea.

Labor is basically a really long workout that you can’t quit in the middle of. ~ Emily Oster PhD

Another medical concern among many pregnant women is labor induction, especially as the age of first-time mothers has increased. Two primary methods for labor induction include Pitocin, used when the cervix shows signs of readiness, and prostaglandins , employed to soften the cervix.

While medical reasons may justify induction, considering potential downsides like more prolonged labor and the need for a cesarean section is vital. Discuss this issue thoroughly with your doctor to weigh the benefits and risks.

chapters.title: The lottery of childbirth
chapters.text:
The third trimester brings anticipation, excitement, and, occasionally, anxiety.

Premature birth becomes a notable worry, prompting discussions about the need for bed rest. However, you must carefully weigh the potential benefits against the risks. Extended bed rest can lead to muscle atrophy, bone loss, and even blood clots. That’s why regular visits to your doctor are vital to check how your pregnancy is progressing and any interventions that might be needed.

The “high-risk” label comes into play for some pregnancies, causing multiple questions and additional worries. Unfortunately, the likelihood of a second difficult pregnancy is high; for example, if a new mother shows signs of obesity after the first baby, the tendency may repeat. Genetic predisposition is also a possibility in this case. Let’s take a look at the most common complications:

• The placenta covering the cervix partially or fully
• Placental detachment from the uterine wall
• Diabetes
• Blood type mismatch between the mother and the child
• Dilation of the cervix
• Fetal growth issues
• High blood pressure

The third trimester involves many medical check-ups. While these visits may seem repetitive, they serve a crucial purpose in monitoring the health of you and your baby.

Pregnancy teaches us to be patient with our bodies and each other.

As your due date approaches, you might feel you’ll be pregnant “forever.” Discomfort tends to intensify, making daily activities more challenging. While it's not uncommon for first-time mothers to enter labor about eight days past their due date, it's crucial to remember that your baby will arrive, and your pregnancy won't last indefinitely.

Finally, as long as you maintain open communication with your obstetrician and prepare mentally and physically for your baby's arrival, you're safe. Recognize that each pregnancy is unique, and your story may differ, which is okay.

Did you know? Modern technologies allow couples to check themselves for genetic disorders before conception. Carrier screening is a popular procedure that detects genetic illnesses based on family history and ethnicity.

chapters.title: To suffer or not to suffer?
chapters.text:
Labor and delivery is the final and most crucial stage of the whole pregnancy. It introduces new mothers to a myriad of choices, both medical and personal, as well as intense emotions.

As labor progresses, it's crucial to understand its phases. Delivery consists of three stages:

1. Dilation: The gradual opening of your cervix up to ten centimeters that can last for days, which is why it's the longest stage.
2. Pushing: Lasting from a few minutes to a few hours.
3. Placenta release: You'll deliver the placenta after your baby is born. However, this is by far the shortest stage.

Another vital thing to consider is using pain relievers during labor. Epidural, or the local anesthesia around a woman's spinal nerves to relieve pain, is a popular procedure most women use in extreme physical pain. Furthermore, the research proves that epidurals are entirely safe for the baby. But what about the mother?

You’re not a bad mother just because you choose to avoid labor pain.

Despite being known for its effectiveness, epidurals have complexities. They can potentially slow labor, forcing doctors to use pitocin, a synthetic oxytocin substitute, to induce contractions. Other risks include low maternal blood pressure and a potential uptick in emergency C-sections. Some theories also link epidurals to babies born the “wrong” way with their faces up. But overall, there's nothing wrong with using this method to relieve pain during labor; the choice is yours.

Another notion to discuss is home birth, which has grown in popularity since the pandemic.

First and foremost, home birth doesn't offer the choice of an epidural. However, some women feel more relaxed in the familiarity of their homes, which may ease the labor and make it more comfortable. Evidence also shows that home birth often leads to easier postpartum recovery.

On the flip side, you never know if something will go wrong. After all, home births are typically attended by midwives rather than physicians. Qualifications and experience levels of midwives can vary significantly, so choosing a reliable one is another responsibility to consider.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Pregnancy is a unique time in a woman's life, differing from case to case. That's why we'll keep discussing this topic as long as humanity continues procreating.

First, let's toss the idea of a strict fertility timeline out the window. Each person's path to parenthood is as unique as a thumbprint, so remain patient when switching from birth control to baby plans.

Whether pregnant or not, being in tune with your body is never in vain. Also, remember that what you eat matters. Staying active is great, but your body may disagree with this as your pregnancy progresses. Either way, exercises like Kegels and prenatal yoga are effective for all women. Paraphrasing the famous saying, in a healthy body, a healthy baby. As you enter the third trimester, keep up with your appointments; regular check-ups are your trusty compass here.

Finally, labor and delivery — the grand finale! We explored the phases of labor, the pros and cons of epidurals, and the rise in home births. We've covered it all; now, the only thing to worry about is the design of your baby's cradle. Now, we leave you to it and hope you'll be more than excited about your new baby's arrival.

Try this


1. For the next chapter of your life, read this Headway summary 一 Bringing Up Bébé: One American Mother Discovering the Wisdom of French Parenting by Pamela Druckerman. The summary delves into the first years of a child’s life, giving helpful and relatable tips to new parents.
2. Don’t deny yourself the things you liked before pregnancy, whether self-care or hobbies. Your life will change when your baby comes, but it’s not a reason to stop being yourself.
